chapters.chapterTitle: How Are We Saved? Grace, Not Works

elements.text:
Most of us arrive at faith carrying a deep instinct: God’s acceptance must be earned. You prayed five times a day. You fasted through Ramadan. You gave to those in need. Each of these was an act of genuine devotion — and somewhere at the back of your mind, each one was also part of the account you were hoping would balance in your favour.

It is a deeply human way to think about God. And in many traditions, it makes complete sense: if the human being is essentially capable — not broken, just forgetful, in need of guidance and discipline — then working harder really ought to be enough. That is a hopeful view of human nature. But the Bible sees the problem differently. And if the problem is different from what we thought, then the solution will be too.

But in this chapter, Jesus and Paul are going to say something that cuts straight across that instinct — something that at first sounds almost too good to be true, and then, slowly, begins to feel like the most liberating thing you have ever heard.

Salvation is not an achievement. It is a gift.

We are not saved because we were good enough. We are saved because God was gracious enough. And that changes everything — including what it means to live well before him.

elements.question: What do you understand by the word grace? Before you go on — think about it for a moment. What picture comes to mind?
elements.answer:
‘Grace’ sounds like God being lenient – turning a blind eye, or grading on a curve. But grace isn’t God lowering his standards. It’s God acting in our favour when we had no right to expect it and nothing to offer in return.

It’s not a reward for the almost-good-enough. It’s a rescue for the lost. It is God’s free gift to us.

elements.passageText:
1And you were dead in your trespasses and sins, 2in which you formerly lived according to this world’s present path, according to the ruler of the kingdom of the air, the spirit now at work in the sons of disobedience, 3among whom all of us also formerly lived out our lives in the cravings of our flesh, indulging the desires of the flesh and the mind, and were by nature children of wrath even as the rest…

(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question: Paul uses striking language here — dead in transgressions and sins. What do you think he means by that? In what sense is a person ‘dead’ before coming to Christ?
Paul says ‘all of us’ lived this way — including himself. Why do you think he is so insistent on including himself in this description?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: Paul was a Pharisee — one of the most religiously devoted people of his day. He had lived an outwardly exemplary life. What does it tell us that even he describes himself as formerly ‘dead’? What was still missing?

elements.text: Ephesians 2 doesn’t stop with the bad news. In fact, Paul seems almost unable to contain himself as he turns to what God has done. Notice how the subject of the sentence changes — not ‘but we tried harder,’ not ‘but we improved.’ But God.

elements.passageText:
4But God, being rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, 5even though we were dead in transgressions, made us alive together with Christ—by grace you are saved!— 6and he raised us up with him and seated us with him in the heavenly realms in Christ Jesus, 7to demonstrate in the coming ages the surpassing wealth of his grace in kindness toward us in Christ Jesus.

(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question: What three things does Paul say about God in verse 4 that explain why he acted? What do those words — mercy, great love, richness — tell us about God’s character?
Paul says God ‘made us alive together with Christ — even though we were dead.’ What does it tell you about grace that God acted when we were dead, not when we were improving?

elements.text: There is a small phrase tucked into the middle of verse 5 that Paul cannot seem to help interrupting himself to say: by grace you are saved! He is so eager to say it that he says it here, before he has even finished the sentence — and then says it again in verse 8. It is as though he cannot let you miss it.

elements.passageText:
8For by grace you are saved through faith, and this is not from yourselves, it is the gift of God; 9it is not from works, so that no one can boast. 10For we are his workmanship, having been created in Christ Jesus for good works that God prepared beforehand so we may do them.

(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question: Paul says salvation is ‘not from yourselves’ and ‘not from works.’ Why does he add ’so that no one can boast’? What problem is he anticipating?

Verse 10 is sometimes missed. Paul does not say works are unimportant — he says we were created for good works. What is the difference between works that earn salvation and works that flow from salvation?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: If good works cannot save us but are still essential to the Christian life, how does that change the motivation behind doing them? What would it feel like to serve God out of love rather than fear of judgment?

elements.text: A Story That Turns Everything Upside Down

Jesus was a master teacher who often used stories to deliver truth more powerfully than a lecture ever could. The parable he tells in Luke 18 was particularly shocking to its original audience — and it is worth letting it be shocking to us too. Two men. One religious, devoted, and serious. One a notorious public sinner. One of them goes home right with God.

It may not be the one you expect.

elements.question: It’s worth reading this short parable in full before you answer the questions below...
elements.answer:
⁹ Jesus also told this parable to some who were confident that they were righteous and looked down on everyone else. ¹⁰ “Two men went up to the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector. ¹¹ The Pharisee stood and prayed about himself like this: ‘God, I thank you that I am not like other people — swindlers, unrighteous people, adulterers — or even like this tax collector. ¹² I fast twice a week; I give a tenth of everything I get.’ ¹³ The tax collector, however, stood far off and would not even look up toward heaven, but beat his breast and said, ‘God, be merciful to me, sinner that I am!’ ¹⁴ I tell you, this man went down to his home justified rather than the Pharisee. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but the one who humbles himself will be exalted.”

(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question: The Pharisee was not lying. He almost certainly did fast twice a week and give a tenth of all he received. What, then, was the problem with his prayer?
The tax collector offers no list of achievements. He asks only for mercy. Jesus says this man went home justified. Why is that so surprising — and so significant?
Jesus says this parable was aimed at ’some who were confident that they were righteous and looked down on everyone else.’ Is that attitude easy to fall into? Have you ever felt it yourself?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: The Pharisee’s prayer was essentially a list of his religious accomplishments. What does it look like when our prayers, without us realising it, become a little like his? What might a prayer shaped by grace sound like instead?

elements.text: Faith, Not Works: What Romans Says

In Romans 4, Paul draws on one of the most respected figures in the whole of religious history — Abraham. The father of the faithful. The one called ‘Friend of God.’ If anyone had earned their standing before God, surely it was Abraham. But Paul makes a startling argument.

elements.passageText:
4Now to the one who works, his pay is not credited due to grace but due to obligation. 5But to the one who does not work, but believes in the one who declares the ungodly righteous, his faith is credited as righteousness.

(NET — see context ↗)

elements.question: Paul uses the language of wages and gifts. What is the difference between a wage and a gift? Which one is salvation, according to this passage?
Paul says God ‘declares the ungodly righteous.’ Does that strike you as strange, or even unfair? What makes it possible for God to do this without ignoring sin?

elements.text:
This is where Chapters 5, 6, and 7 fit together. Chapter 5 showed us the depth of the problem — sin runs deeper than outward behaviour; it is a condition of the heart. Chapter 6 showed us the answer — Jesus took that sin upon himself at the cross, and the resurrection declares that his death achieved everything God intended. And now, in Chapter 7, we see how that applies to us: by grace, through faith. Not by performance. Through trust.

The cross is what makes it possible for God to declare sinners righteous without ignoring sin. The penalty has been paid. The gift is free.
So What Does This Mean for Assurance?

For many people — especially those who have grown up in a tradition that emphasises judgment and uncertainty — one of the most unsettling aspects of the Christian gospel is this: you can know that you are saved.

In some frameworks, the verdict on a person’s life is hidden until the very end — revealed only when deeds are weighed and the final accounting is made. No one can know in advance how that will go. The faithful can only hope, and keep trying.

The Christian gospel offers something different — not because it takes sin less seriously, but because it takes the cross more seriously. Assurance is not based on the weight of your deeds. It is based on what Christ has already done, and on God’s own promise. Some of you may have hoped that the Prophet would intercede for you on that final day. The Christian claim is that Jesus is himself both Judge and Saviour — and that his intercession is not a hope but a certainty, secured by what he has already accomplished.

Not arrogantly. Not because you are better than others. But because you are trusting a promise made by God himself, sealed in the blood of Christ, and confirmed by the Holy Spirit.

This is not a claim about your goodness. It is a claim about his faithfulness.

For many people, the longing for this kind of assurance is not primarily intellectual. It is the quiet exhaustion of a life spent trying to be enough — praying enough, obeying enough, atoning enough — and never quite knowing if the account will balance. That exhaustion is real. The gospel does not dismiss it. It addresses it: not by demanding more effort, but by offering a certainty that effort could never produce.

elements.question: How do you feel about the idea of being certain of God’s acceptance? Does it feel freeing, or does it make you uneasy? Why?

In some religious traditions, no one can know until the Day of Judgment whether God has truly accepted them — the verdict is hidden until the final reckoning. What does it mean to you that Christian assurance is something you can know now, not something you have to wait to find out?
If assurance is based on God’s promise and not on our performance, what happens to that assurance when we sin? Does it have to be re-earned?

deeper.label: Go deeper …
deeper.question: John writes: “I have written these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God so that you may know that you have eternal life” (1 John 5:13). Why do you think John considers it important that we know, not merely hope? What difference would that certainty make to the way you pray, or live?

elements.text:
Three truths to hold clearly from this chapter:

Grace is not God lowering his standards. It is God, at great cost to himself, meeting us where we could never reach him. The cross shows that sin was taken seriously — completely seriously — and yet forgiven completely.

Faith is not a religious work we perform to earn God’s favour. It is the open hand that receives a gift. It is trust. It is dependence. It is saying: I cannot do this. I am relying entirely on what Christ has done.

Works matter — enormously. But they flow from salvation; they do not produce it. Ephesians 2:10 says we are created in Christ Jesus for good works. We do not work in order to be accepted. We work because we are accepted.

elements.term: “Doesn’t this mean we can do whatever we want?”
elements.question: If we’re saved by grace and not by works, doesn’t that remove any reason to live morally? Think about it before tapping ’see response.’
elements.answer: Paul addresses this directly in Romans 6. Grace is not a licence for careless living — it is a power that changes us from the inside. Those who have truly received grace find that their desires begin to change. They want to please God — not to earn his favour, but because they love him. A child who knows they are truly loved by their parent does not think: ‘Great, I can do anything.’ They want to make their parent glad. Grace produces that kind of love.

elements.question: Is it proud to claim assurance of salvation?
elements.answer: Christian assurance is not based on our own goodness — if it were, it would indeed be arrogant. It is based entirely on God’s promise in Christ. Trusting a promise is not pride; it is faith. If a trusted friend promises to meet you and you say ‘I trust that he will come,’ that is not arrogance — it is confidence in his reliability, not your own. Christian assurance is the same: it rests on what God has promised, not on what we have achieved. This is quite different from any framework where the outcome remains hidden until the final day. Here, the verdict has already been declared — in the resurrection of Jesus.

elements.term: “What about good deeds — do they count for nothing?”
elements.question: Does grace make good deeds irrelevant?
elements.answer: Not at all. Ephesians 2:10 says we are ‘created in Christ Jesus to do good works.’ Good deeds matter enormously — but they are the fruit of salvation, not its root. Think of it this way: a tree does not produce fruit in order to become a fruit tree. It produces fruit because it already is one. In the same way, good works do not make us acceptable to God — they flow from those who have already been made acceptable, by grace.

elements.question: Which of these three things has most challenged or encouraged you in this chapter — the meaning of grace, the role of faith, or the place of good works? Why?
Is there an area of your life where you have been trying to earn God’s acceptance rather than receive it? What would it look like to approach that area differently?
If you fully believed that God already accepts you completely — not because of what you have done, but because of what Christ has done — what would change?

elements.text:
This chapter has been about the very heart of the gospel.

We came in as people who could not save ourselves — not because we hadn’t tried, but because the problem ran deeper than effort could reach. And we encountered a God who, at the cost of his own Son, did what we could not do. He reached across the gap. He paid what we owed. He declared us righteous — not because we earned it, but because Jesus had.

That is grace. The only fitting response to a gift this extraordinary is not to try to earn it — it is simply to receive it, with open hands, and live as those who know they are loved.

elements.term: Something to carry with you this week
elements.question: Take one of the three key passages from this chapter — Ephesians 2:1–10, Luke 18:9–14, or Romans 4:4–5 — and read it slowly once a day. As you do, ask yourself: Am I approaching God today with open hands, or with a list?

elements.text:
Close by praying together. You might use this as a guide:

Father, thank you that you did not wait for us to be good enough before you acted. Thank you for the grace you showed us in Christ — that while we were still far off, you came toward us. Teach us to receive that gift — not to grasp after it, and not to take it for granted, but to rest in it, and to live from it. Amen.
